utterances: Good afternoon.
And you wrote in a post at politico.com that you have been shocked by the disgust with which prominent Republicans in every state have simply had it with what our party has become.

Yeah, well I think the thing that - the significant about Colin Powell's endorsement of Obama is that it comes after a long list of other prominent Republicans and Conservatives, who have expressed their dismay about either the Bush administration or the McCain campaign.

And so, Powell is only giving voice to a lot of people out there, who agree that, you know, that the party has just gone off the tracks and - you know, Neal when this started, the problem was George W.
Bush, and he was very unpopular.
And it was just John McCain's bad fortune to be the Republican Nominee, while Bush was in the White House.
But that's changed, and now there's a reaction against McCain himself.
He has been erratic, he has - his campaign has been very mean-spirited and talking about a lot of things.
Here you just play this piece here.
Yes, OK, everybody knows he's a liberal.
That is - doesn't seem to be what people are very concerned about today.
Interesting that you talk about a lot of the intellectuals within the Republican Party, who have seemingly recoiled in the last several weeks in particular.
Is there an anti-intellectualism in the Republican Party?
Well, you know, Bill Crystal talked about that a little bit this morning in The New York Times, although he sort of celebrated that fact.
There is an anti-intellectualism, and Sarah Palin gives voice to that.
It's certainly not true.
You know, I've taught at Harvard and Princeton.

It's certainly not true that - you know, that anybody who's there is automatically smarter than the people on main street, but you know, the fact of the matter is, you know, that we should celebrate the intellect.

We should celebrate ideas, and not demean them and dismiss them, and you know, I would never have thought that the Republican Party, which we prided ourselves for so many years on being the party of ideas, which suddenly have become the party of reaction against thoughtfulness, against reflection.
The ideas we're also easy to summarize.
The Republican Party was smaller government, lower taxes, and spending on defense.
Well, it was not so much smaller government, that's now become the litany, but it was limited government, limited to what the constitution permitted.
But it was for other things that not - you know, we were for peace through strength.
We wanted a strong military to keep the peace, not to find ways to go to war.

We celebrated individual liberties and limits on government, so we were not in favor - we'd never been in favor in the past of a president proclaiming that as the commander-in-chief, he has all these unlimited powers.
So it's been a lot of changes from what we used to say.
The party has become, as Colin Powell said, much narrower.
And it's dealing a lot in irrelevancies.
Like what?
Well, you know, for example, Bill Ayers was doing what he did, and it was a very long time ago when Obama was eight years old.
It was reprehensible.
I don't know that he has really changed.

But on the other hand, you know, I don't know that I want to be held responsible for anything that might have been done by anybody whoever had a campaign event for me, when I was running for Congress.
You know, the people are concerned about the economy, they're concerned about the Iraq war, they're concerned about competent management of government.
And one of the things that concerns me is that so many people in Congress on the Republican ticket or in the White House, seem to be anti-government.
Well, if you're anti-government, don't run for office.
You know, the idea - we're self government, this is not a dictatorship, this is not the czar.
You know, it's self government, and I believe in self government.
Well, both candidates seem to be running against Washington.
Well, they're running against Washington in different ways though.
They're both saying they're going to bring reform.
And you know, I agree with both of them.
You know, actually, here I agree probably more with McCain, because McCain thinks the change the people want is less corruption in government.
Obama seems to think, you know, the people want to move to the left.
I don't think people want to move to the left.
So, I don't agree with that.

But I do think overall, there has been way too much focus in the McCain campaign on not only things like whether it's Ayers or other things that perhaps in - I mean, when I watch - Neal, when I watch a rally in which Republicans attending McCain's rallies, you know, are shouting the nastiness that they shout at Obama.
You know, it makes me cringe.
I mean, that's not what we've ever been about.
And I think we need to get a grip, and go back to what our principles are, and why we want to have a government that is responsive to the people.
You know, we've gotten into this bumper-sticker war, and the bumper stickers are more and more meaningless coming out of the McCain campaign.
Do you see if this is the legacy of the Lee Atwaters of the world, the people who said - found a formula, said we can attack our opponent's patriotism, and that it will work every time?
Well, I don't know if I would go back to Atwater, you know.
I knew Lee and it's possible that he would have approved this.
Certainly as a legacy of Newt Gingrich and Karl Rove, I mean, we've seen that.
And it's the legacy of George W.
Bush, who tends to see everybody as either allies or enemies.
Let's get some callers on the line, our guest a former congressman from Oklahoma, Mickey Edwards.
And we're asking Republicans to call us today with their reaction to the Colin Powell endorsement of Senator Barack Obama, the Democratic presidential candidate.
And we'll begin in Morrisville, North Carolina.
Nick is on the line.
I just want to say that I agree 100 percent with the colonel's decision.
Being a Republican, I was faced with a tough decision the past week or two, it's been weighing on my mind.
And I've decided the same thing.
I mean, just due to this negative campaign, and a lot of it has to do with the vice-presidential nominee.
I mean, I think it was very - you know, he drew a line in the sand and, you know, decided which direction he wanted to go with the party.
And that's really left a bad taste in my mouth, and I don't - I mean, I question whether I'm a Republican anymore, I'm just left without a party.
So it's - I'm glad that there's others out there that feel the same way as I do.
Interesting.
Mickey Edwards, all those years ago Ronald Reagan said it was the - he didn't leave the Democratic Party, the Democratic Party left him.
Well, that's true.
And if I can just pick up on there for one second, Neal.
You know, the famous quote from Ronald Reagan was that government's not the solution, government's the problem.
Well, in my book, you know, I talk about that.

And, you know, what he said was, in the current circumstance, meaning the Carter administration, government was the problem, but he went on in that same sentence - same paragraph to say, you know, we're not anti-government, we're for a government that stands with the people and that creates opportunity.
So, a lot of the people who now - who claimed to be Reagan Republicans, don't have any idea what a Reagan Republican really is.

Mickey Edwards mentioned his book, it's called ""Reclaiming Conservatism: How a Great American Political Movement Got Lost--And How It Can Find Its Way Back."" Nick, are you planning to vote November fourth?
Of course.
And I think I finally made my decision strong.
I know, as a fervent McCain supporter before, but you know, I think that I'm going to have to go the other way.
Nick, thanks very much for the call.
Thank you.
And as you look at states like North Carolina where Nick just called from and Virginia, Republicans in an ordinary year would not have too much trouble winning those states.
Yeah.

One of the things that's so significant here in this campaign is that - that the states that traditionally go for the Democrats in a presidential race are going again for, you know, maybe even by bigger margins this time, and going to sweep in a lot of congressional Democrats as well.
And John McCain is having to fight desperately to just to hold on to those states that have always voted for Republicans, the states that George Bush carried last time, are now in play.
I mean, who would've thought at the beginning of this, that John McCain was going to have to fight to try to keep Virginia, but that's exactly the case.
Let's get another caller, this is Jessie.
Jessie with us from Granger in another surprise battleground state, Indiana.
Hi there.
Hi, Jessie.
Yeah.
I'm a Republican, I voted for Obama already.
I voted early, because I actually signed up to volunteer for McCain's campaign, but every time I got an email, it was just - it wasn't about the issue, it was about just stupid stuff regarding Obama.
Should we put this song, with this picture of Obama's face in a heart?
You know, talk about the issue.
You know, we have problems here in America.
And I just, you know, Obama, he just reaches people, and he really, really cares about what everyone's going through.
Not just the top five percent.
You know, he cares about everyone.
And what kind of town is Granger?
And do other people there feel the same way, do you think?
Oh, no. Granger is a Republican - it's a little village.
I live on Republican sorrow, and so I'm like out of the ordinary in my neighborhood right now.
So not too many Obama lawn signs down there?
No.
All right.
Nope.
